blocks.content: They asked the doctor about children on a Tuesday afternoon in early spring, fourteen months into the marriage.
The doctor was a good one — the kind who answered questions fully and didn't perform optimism she didn't have. She sat across from both of them and she laid it out plainly.

Possible. Not impossible. But the condition created compounding risk during pregnancy — elevated stress on the heart, medication adjustments that carried their own uncertainties, delivery as a period of particular concern. The odds were not insurmountable. But they were real.
And then she asked the question.

blocks.speaker: Doctor
blocks.content: There's something I want both of you to sit with, not just as a medical question. If something were to go wrong — not because we didn't try, but because these things sometimes go wrong — is that something you could carry? Both of you. On your consciences. Knowing you made the choice with full information.
Cassandra looked at her hands. Ethan looked at the doctor, then at Cassandra.
They didn't answer right away. They weren't supposed to.

They talked about it for weeks afterward. In the kitchen. In bed before sleep. On a Sunday walk around the neighborhood when the silence between them was the right kind — working silence, thinking silence.

What they arrived at, slowly and without drama, was this: they were not closed to it. They were not rushing toward it. If it happened, they would meet it with everything they had. If it didn't — if the body decided no, or if they decided the risk was more than they wanted to ask of each other — that was a complete life too. They already had a complete life.
Cassandra told her mother on the phone. Her mother was quiet for a moment.
Then she said: whatever you decide, we are with you.
That was the end of the conversation and the beginning of the peace.
